title: Chronic Hepatitis B
oneLiner: Chronic HBV infection (HBsAg+ >6mo); leading cause of cirrhosis and HCC worldwide; often silent until decompensation or cancer.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute flare / decompensated chronic HBV** – rapid rise in bilirubin, INR >1.5, new ascites/encephalopathy. The single finding: **asterixis** or **reduced GCS without another cause**.
- **Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) with rupture or portal vein thrombosis** – sudden severe RUQ pain, shock, rising LDH/AFP. The single finding: **new tender hepatomegaly + haemodynamic instability**.

Rule out both before assuming a stable chronic carrier.
sections.Presentation:
- Often asymptomatic (found on screening / blood bank check)
- Fatigue, malaise, right upper quadrant discomfort
- Jaundice, dark urine, pale stools (if flare)
- Loss of appetite, nausea, arthralgias (prodrome)
- Exam: hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, spider naevi, palmar erythema, caput medusae (if cirrhosis), ascites, peripheral oedema, gynaecomastia, testicular atrophy
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Other chronic hepatitis**:
  - Hepatitis C (anti-HCV+; HBsAg-)
  - Autoimmune hepatitis (ANA+, IgG raised, liver biopsy interface hepatitis)
  - Alcoholic liver disease (AST>ALT >2:1, heavy alcohol history)
  - NASH (metabolic syndrome, steatosis on ultrasound)
- **Drug-induced liver injury** (temporal association, eosinophilia, DILI score)
- **Wilson disease** (<40yo, Kayser-Fleischer rings, low caeruloplasmin)
- **Splitter:** **HBsAg positive >6 months** confirms chronic HBV; HBeAg and HBV DNA help differentiate active vs. inactive carrier.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** urine dip for bilirubin/urobilinogen; check for asterixis; caput medusae (variceal pattern); ascites thrill/shifting dullness
- **Bloods:**
  - LFTs (ALT, AST, ALP, GGT, bilirubin, albumin)
  - Clotting screen (INR, APTT)
  - FBC, U&E, creatinine, CRP
  - **HBV serology:** HBsAg, HBsAb, HBeAg, HBeAb, anti-HBc IgM/IgG
  - **HBV DNA viral load** (quantitative)
  - AFP (screening for HCC)
  - Anti-HDV (if risk factors – IVDU, endemic area)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** abdominal ultrasound with doppler – liver echotexture, spleen size, portal vein patency, exclude HCC
  - **Second-line:** transient elastography (FibroScan) for fibrosis; CT/MRI for HCC characterisation
- **Special tests:** liver biopsy (rarely needed if elastography/AFP equivocal)
- **Never-miss test:** **HBV DNA viral load** – determines treatment eligibility and risk of progression. Also **AFP** (baseline for HCC surveillance).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If acute flare/decompensation: admit, check INR, bilirubin, GCS, fluid balance
  - Exclude hepatitis A/E co-infection (anti-HAV IgM, anti-HEV IgM)
  - Stop any hepatotoxic drugs (NSAIDs, paracetamol >2g/d, amoxicillin-clavulanate)
  - Start supportive care: IV fluids (avoid overfilling), correct hypoglycaemia
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Start oral antiviral if:** high HBV DNA (>2000 IU/mL) + raised ALT, or significant fibrosis (F2+), or decompensation
  - First-line: **Tenofovir disoproxil** 245mg daily **or** **Entecavir** 0.5mg daily (nucleoside-naïve)
  - Adjust dose for renal impairment ([check local policy] – eGFR <50)
  - Monitor ALT, bilirubin, INR daily during flare
  - Consider IV N-acetylcysteine for severe acute-on-chronic liver failure (ACLF) – [check local protocol]
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Indefinite antiviral suppression (lifelong for most)
  - HCC surveillance: **6-monthly ultrasound + AFP**
  - Vaccinate household and sexual contacts (if HBsAb negative)
  - Avoid alcohol, NSAIDs, and herbal hepatotoxins
- **Default Orders (if admitted for decompensation):**
  - Low-salt diet (if ascites) – 2g Na/d
  - Daily weight, strict fluid balance
  - Lactulose if encephalopathic (15–30ml TDS adjusted to 2–3 soft stools/d)
  - Vitamin K 10mg IV once if INR >2 (may not help – treat cause)
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH only if platelets >50 and no active bleeding (with cirrhosis risk is uncertain – [check local policy])
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line oral antivirals**
  - **Tenofovir disoproxil fumarate (Viread)** 245mg PO once daily
  - **Tenofovir alafenamide (Vemlidy)** 25mg PO once daily (better renal/bone profile)
  - **Entecavir (Baraclude)** 0.5mg PO once daily (for nucleoside-naïve); 1mg if lamivudine-resistant
  - Duration: indefinite; consider stopping only after HBeAg seroconversion + undetectable HBV DNA >12mo (hepatology decision)
- **Alternatives**
  - Pegylated interferon alfa-2a (Pegasys) – 180mcg SC weekly for 48 weeks (in selected young, HBeAg+, low DNA, no cirrhosis) – [check local policy]
  - Lamivudine (Zeffix) 100mg daily – no longer first-line due to high resistance
- **Key contraindications**
  - Tenofovir disoproxil: avoid if eGFR <30 or combine with nephrotoxic drugs
  - Entecavir: dose reduce if CrCl <50 mL/min
  - Interferon: avoid in decompensated cirrhosis, severe depression, autoimmune disease
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment**
  - Tenofovir disoproxil: reduce dose if eGFR <50; avoid if <30
  - Entecavir: reduce dose if CrCl <50 (e.g. 0.5mg every 48h)
- **Most dangerous dose error**
  - **Not adjusting tenofovir for renal impairment** – can cause Fanconi syndrome, acute renal failure, osteomalacia
  - Starting lamivudine alone (instead of entecavir/tenofovir) – high resistance risk
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Acute-on-chronic liver failure (ACLF) – jaundice, coagulopathy, encephalopathy, ascites
  - Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) – suspect if new ascites + fever/abdominal pain
  - Variceal haemorrhage (if underlying cirrhosis)
  - Hepatorenal syndrome – acute kidney injury with no other cause
- **Chronic:**
  - Cirrhosis (with portal hypertension)
  - Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC)
  - Extrahepatic: polyarteritis nodosa, cryoglobulinaemia, membranous glomerulonephritis, aplastic anaemia (rare)
sections.Prognosis:
- Without treatment: 15–40% develop cirrhosis; HCC risk 2–5%/yr once cirrhotic
- With effective antiviral suppression: near-normal life expectancy; HCC risk reduced but not eliminated
- HBsAg seroclearance (functional cure) occurs in ~1–3%/yr – associated with excellent prognosis
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
1. **Screen all household contacts and sexual partners** – vaccinate if HBsAb negative (within 24h of exposure if known)
2. **Monitor ALT and HBV DNA every 3–6 months** in inactive carriers – they can reactivate
3. **Always check AFP + ultrasound every 6 months** in all HBsAg+ patients (even with normal LFTs) – HCC can develop without cirrhosis
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
1. **Starting steroids for presumed autoimmune hepatitis without HBV serology** – can cause fatal viral reactivation
2. **Abruptly stopping antivirals** – triggers severe acute flare (hepatic decompensation)
3. **Forgetting to check for hepatitis D (HDV)** in at-risk patients (IVDU, migrants from sub-Saharan Africa/Amazon) – co-infection accelerates cirrhosis
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** jaundice worsens, new confusion or drowsiness, abdominal distension (ascites), vomiting blood or passing dark stools
- **Avoid:** alcohol, NSAIDs, >2g paracetamol/day, raw shellfish (risk of Vibrio)
- **Follow-up:** hepatology clinic within **4 weeks** (or sooner if flare); GP to arrange 6-monthly surveillance scans
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / hepatology reg immediately if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L
  - GCS <15 or new asterixis
  - INR >2.0 and rising
  - New ascites with respiratory compromise or suspected SBP
  - Variceal haemorrhage (haematemesis/melaena) – start terlipressin + antibiotics, arrange emergency OGD
  - Acute kidney injury (creatinine rise >50% from baseline)
- **Consider ITU referral for:**
  - ACLF with organ failure (ventilation, vasopressors, RRT)
  – GCS <8 or status epilepticus (encephalopathy)
  - Haemodynamic instability despite resuscitation

rawMarkdown:
## Chronic Hepatitis B

**One-liner:** Chronic HBV infection (HBsAg+ >6mo); leading cause of cirrhosis and HCC worldwide; often silent until decompensation or cancer.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?

- **Acute flare / decompensated chronic HBV** – rapid rise in bilirubin, INR >1.5, new ascites/encephalopathy. The single finding: **asterixis** or **reduced GCS without another cause**.
- **Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) with rupture or portal vein thrombosis** – sudden severe RUQ pain, shock, rising LDH/AFP. The single finding: **new tender hepatomegaly + haemodynamic instability**.

Rule out both before assuming a stable chronic carrier.

### Presentation

- Often asymptomatic (found on screening / blood bank check)
- Fatigue, malaise, right upper quadrant discomfort
- Jaundice, dark urine, pale stools (if flare)
- Loss of appetite, nausea, arthralgias (prodrome)
- Exam: hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, spider naevi, palmar erythema, caput medusae (if cirrhosis), ascites, peripheral oedema, gynaecomastia, testicular atrophy

### Differential Diagnosis

- **Other chronic hepatitis**:
  - Hepatitis C (anti-HCV+; HBsAg-)
  - Autoimmune hepatitis (ANA+, IgG raised, liver biopsy interface hepatitis)
  - Alcoholic liver disease (AST>ALT >2:1, heavy alcohol history)
  - NASH (metabolic syndrome, steatosis on ultrasound)
- **Drug-induced liver injury** (temporal association, eosinophilia, DILI score)
- **Wilson disease** (<40yo, Kayser-Fleischer rings, low caeruloplasmin)
- **Splitter:** **HBsAg positive >6 months** confirms chronic HBV; HBeAg and HBV DNA help differentiate active vs. inactive carrier.

### Investigations

- **Bedside:** urine dip for bilirubin/urobilinogen; check for asterixis; caput medusae (variceal pattern); ascites thrill/shifting dullness
- **Bloods:**
  - LFTs (ALT, AST, ALP, GGT, bilirubin, albumin)
  - Clotting screen (INR, APTT)
  - FBC, U&E, creatinine, CRP
  - **HBV serology:** HBsAg, HBsAb, HBeAg, HBeAb, anti-HBc IgM/IgG
  - **HBV DNA viral load** (quantitative)
  - AFP (screening for HCC)
  - Anti-HDV (if risk factors – IVDU, endemic area)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** abdominal ultrasound with doppler – liver echotexture, spleen size, portal vein patency, exclude HCC
  - **Second-line:** transient elastography (FibroScan) for fibrosis; CT/MRI for HCC characterisation
- **Special tests:** liver biopsy (rarely needed if elastography/AFP equivocal)
- **Never-miss test:** **HBV DNA viral load** – determines treatment eligibility and risk of progression. Also **AFP** (baseline for HCC surveillance).

### Management

- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If acute flare/decompensation: admit, check INR, bilirubin, GCS, fluid balance
  - Exclude hepatitis A/E co-infection (anti-HAV IgM, anti-HEV IgM)
  - Stop any hepatotoxic drugs (NSAIDs, paracetamol >2g/d, amoxicillin-clavulanate)
  - Start supportive care: IV fluids (avoid overfilling), correct hypoglycaemia
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Start oral antiviral if:** high HBV DNA (>2000 IU/mL) + raised ALT, or significant fibrosis (F2+), or decompensation
  - First-line: **Tenofovir disoproxil** 245mg daily **or** **Entecavir** 0.5mg daily (nucleoside-naïve)
  - Adjust dose for renal impairment ([check local policy] – eGFR <50)
  - Monitor ALT, bilirubin, INR daily during flare
  - Consider IV N-acetylcysteine for severe acute-on-chronic liver failure (ACLF) – [check local protocol]
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Indefinite antiviral suppression (lifelong for most)
  - HCC surveillance: **6-monthly ultrasound + AFP**
  - Vaccinate household and sexual contacts (if HBsAb negative)
  - Avoid alcohol, NSAIDs, and herbal hepatotoxins
- **Default Orders (if admitted for decompensation):**
  - Low-salt diet (if ascites) – 2g Na/d
  - Daily weight, strict fluid balance
  - Lactulose if encephalopathic (15–30ml TDS adjusted to 2–3 soft stools/d)
  - Vitamin K 10mg IV once if INR >2 (may not help – treat cause)
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH only if platelets >50 and no active bleeding (with cirrhosis risk is uncertain – [check local policy])

### Drugs & Doses

- **First-line oral antivirals**
  - **Tenofovir disoproxil fumarate (Viread)** 245mg PO once daily
  - **Tenofovir alafenamide (Vemlidy)** 25mg PO once daily (better renal/bone profile)
  - **Entecavir (Baraclude)** 0.5mg PO once daily (for nucleoside-naïve); 1mg if lamivudine-resistant
  - Duration: indefinite; consider stopping only after HBeAg seroconversion + undetectable HBV DNA >12mo (hepatology decision)
- **Alternatives**
  - Pegylated interferon alfa-2a (Pegasys) – 180mcg SC weekly for 48 weeks (in selected young, HBeAg+, low DNA, no cirrhosis) – [check local policy]
  - Lamivudine (Zeffix) 100mg daily – no longer first-line due to high resistance
- **Key contraindications**
  - Tenofovir disoproxil: avoid if eGFR <30 or combine with nephrotoxic drugs
  - Entecavir: dose reduce if CrCl <50 mL/min
  - Interferon: avoid in decompensated cirrhosis, severe depression, autoimmune disease
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment**
  - Tenofovir disoproxil: reduce dose if eGFR <50; avoid if <30
  - Entecavir: reduce dose if CrCl <50 (e.g. 0.5mg every 48h)
- **Most dangerous dose error**
  - **Not adjusting tenofovir for renal impairment** – can cause Fanconi syndrome, acute renal failure, osteomalacia
  - Starting lamivudine alone (instead of entecavir/tenofovir) – high resistance risk

### Complications

- **Acute:**
  - Acute-on-chronic liver failure (ACLF) – jaundice, coagulopathy, encephalopathy, ascites
  - Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) – suspect if new ascites + fever/abdominal pain
  - Variceal haemorrhage (if underlying cirrhosis)
  - Hepatorenal syndrome – acute kidney injury with no other cause
- **Chronic:**
  - Cirrhosis (with portal hypertension)
  - Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC)
  - Extrahepatic: polyarteritis nodosa, cryoglobulinaemia, membranous glomerulonephritis, aplastic anaemia (rare)

### Prognosis

- Without treatment: 15–40% develop cirrhosis; HCC risk 2–5%/yr once cirrhotic
- With effective antiviral suppression: near-normal life expectancy; HCC risk reduced but not eliminated
- HBsAg seroclearance (functional cure) occurs in ~1–3%/yr – associated with excellent prognosis

### Pearls (positive actions)

1. **Screen all household contacts and sexual partners** – vaccinate if HBsAb negative (within 24h of exposure if known)
2. **Monitor ALT and HBV DNA every 3–6 months** in inactive carriers – they can reactivate
3. **Always check AFP + ultrasound every 6 months** in all HBsAg+ patients (even with normal LFTs) – HCC can develop without cirrhosis

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)

1. **Starting steroids for presumed autoimmune hepatitis without HBV serology** – can cause fatal viral reactivation
2. **Abruptly stopping antivirals** – triggers severe acute flare (hepatic decompensation)
3. **Forgetting to check for hepatitis D (HDV)** in at-risk patients (IVDU, migrants from sub-Saharan Africa/Amazon) – co-infection accelerates cirrhosis

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)

- **Return if:** jaundice worsens, new confusion or drowsiness, abdominal distension (ascites), vomiting blood or passing dark stools
- **Avoid:** alcohol, NSAIDs, >2g paracetamol/day, raw shellfish (risk of Vibrio)
- **Follow-up:** hepatology clinic within **4 weeks** (or sooner if flare); GP to arrange 6-monthly surveillance scans

### Escalation Criteria

- **Call senior / hepatology reg immediately if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L
  - GCS <15 or new asterixis
  - INR >2.0 and rising
  - New ascites with respiratory compromise or suspected SBP
  - Variceal haemorrhage (haematemesis/melaena) – start terlipressin + antibiotics, arrange emergency OGD
  - Acute kidney injury (creatinine rise >50% from baseline)
- **Consider ITU referral for:**
  - ACLF with organ failure (ventilation, vasopressors, RRT)
  – GCS <8 or status epilepticus (encephalopathy)
  - Haemodynamic instability despite resuscitation
